---
title: "EpiFlow D3"
subtitle: "User Guide — Practical Walkthrough"
author: "Serrano Laboratory - Boston University School of Medicine"
date: today
format:
  pdf:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    number-sections: true
    pdf-engine: xelatex
    documentclass: article
    geometry: 
      - margin=1in
      - top=1.2in
    include-in-header:
      text: |
        \usepackage{fontspec}
        \renewcommand{\familydefault}{\sfdefault}

        \IfFontExistsTF{Helvetica Neue}{
          \setsansfont{Helvetica Neue}[Scale = 0.95]
          \setmainfont{Helvetica Neue}[Scale = 0.95]
        }{
          \IfFontExistsTF{Arial}{
            \setsansfont{Arial}[Scale = 0.95]
            \setmainfont{Arial}[Scale = 0.95]
          }{
            \usepackage{helvet}
            \renewcommand{\familydefault}{\sfdefault}
          }
        }

        \setmonofont{Courier New}[Scale=0.85]
        
        \usepackage{fancyhdr}
        \usepackage{lastpage}
        \pagestyle{fancy}
        \fancyhf{}
        \fancyhead[L]{\footnotesize\color{gray}\textbf{EPIFLOW D3 USER GUIDE}}
        \fancyhead[R]{\footnotesize\color{gray}\leftmark}
        \fancyfoot[C]{\footnotesize\color{gray}Page \thepage\ of \pageref{LastPage}}
        \renewcommand{\headrulewidth}{0.5pt}
        \setlength{\headheight}{14pt}
        
        % Colors — Okabe-Ito accessible palette
        \usepackage[table,dvipsnames]{xcolor}
        \definecolor{oiblue}{HTML}{0072B2}
        \definecolor{oivermillion}{HTML}{D55E00}
        \definecolor{oigreen}{HTML}{009E73}
        \definecolor{oiorange}{HTML}{E69F00}
        \definecolor{oiskyblue}{HTML}{56B4E9}
        \definecolor{oipurple}{HTML}{CC79A7}
        \definecolor{darkgray}{HTML}{333333}
        \definecolor{mediumgray}{HTML}{666666}
        \definecolor{lightgray}{HTML}{F7F7F7}
        
        \usepackage{titlesec}
        \titleformat{\section}
          {\color{oiblue}\normalfont\LARGE\bfseries}
          {\thesection}{1em}{}[\color{oiblue}\titlerule]
        \titleformat{\subsection}
          {\color{darkgray}\normalfont\Large\bfseries}
          {\thesubsection}{0.8em}{}
        \titleformat{\subsubsection}
          {\color{mediumgray}\normalfont\large\bfseries}
          {\thesubsubsection}{0.8em}{}
        
        \usepackage{booktabs}
        \usepackage{array}
        \usepackage{colortbl}
        \usepackage{longtable}
        \arrayrulecolor{mediumgray}
        \renewcommand{\arraystretch}{1.3}
        \rowcolors{2}{white}{lightgray}
        
        \usepackage{tcolorbox}
        \tcbuselibrary{skins,breakable}
        
        \newtcolorbox{infobox}[1][]{
          colback=oiskyblue!10,
          colframe=oiblue,
          colbacktitle=oiblue,
          coltitle=white,
          fonttitle=\bfseries,
          title=#1,
          boxrule=1pt,
          arc=3pt,
          breakable
        }
        
        \newtcolorbox{warningbox}[1][]{
          colback=oiorange!10,
          colframe=oiorange,
          colbacktitle=oiorange,
          coltitle=white,
          fonttitle=\bfseries,
          title=#1,
          boxrule=1pt,
          arc=3pt,
          breakable
        }
        
        \newtcolorbox{tipbox}[1][]{
          colback=oigreen!10,
          colframe=oigreen,
          colbacktitle=oigreen,
          coltitle=white,
          fonttitle=\bfseries,
          title=#1,
          boxrule=1pt,
          arc=3pt,
          breakable
        }
        
        \usepackage{enumitem}
        \setlist[itemize]{leftmargin=*, itemsep=2pt, parsep=2pt}
        \setlist[enumerate]{leftmargin=*, itemsep=2pt, parsep=2pt}
---

\newpage

# Getting Started

## What is EpiFlow D3?

EpiFlow D3 is a web-based platform for analyzing spectral flow cytometry data, built for simultaneous quantification of multiple histone H3 post-translational modifications (PTMs) with single-cell and cell-cycle resolution. It runs in your browser at:

\begin{center}
\textbf{\textcolor{oiblue}{https://epiflow.serranolab.org}}
\end{center}

## Typical Workflow

```text
Upload .rds --> Filter --> Explore --> Analyze --> Export
     |             |          |           |          |
  OMIQ data    Genotype   Overview    LMM stats   SVG/PNG
  converter    Identity   Ridge       PCA/UMAP    CSV tables
               Phase      Violin      ML/RF       HTML report
               Metadata   Heatmap     Clustering   Sessions
```

## Preparing Your Data

EpiFlow D3 accepts `.rds` files in long format. Use the **OMIQ $\rightarrow$ EpiFlow Converter** Shiny app to transform raw OMIQ CSV exports into this format.

### Required Columns

| Column | Description | Example Values |
|--------|-------------|----------------|
| `cell_id` | Unique cell identifier | 1, 2, 3, ... |
| `genotype` | Primary comparison group | WT, KO |
| `replicate` | Biological replicate | R1, R2, R3 |
| `identity` | Cell population / gate | G0G1, G2 Phase |
| `cell_cycle` | Cell cycle phase | G0/G1, S, G2, G2/M, M |
| `H3PTM` | Histone modification name | H3K27ac, H3K4me1 |
| `value` | Fluorescence intensity | 5.83, 6.12 |

### Optional Columns

| Column | Description |
|--------|-------------|
| `FxCycle` | DNA channel intensity |
| `phH3` | Phospho-H3 intensity |
| `timepoint` | Timepoint metadata |
| `condition` | Treatment/condition metadata |
| `cell_type` | Cell type metadata |
| Phenotypic markers | Any numeric columns (PAX6, Ki67, NeuN, etc.) |

Optional columns appear as additional grouping/stratification variables throughout the platform.

\newpage

# Upload and Filter

## Uploading Data

1. Open **https://epiflow.serranolab.org** in your browser
2. Click **Choose .rds File** and select your EpiFlow-format file
3. The platform loads and displays: detected H3 markers, phenotypic markers, genotypes, identities, cell cycle phases, and any additional metadata columns

\begin{infobox}[Supported File Size]
The platform handles files up to 200 MB. For very large datasets (500K+ cells), initial upload may take 10--20 seconds. All subsequent analyses use the server-side data.
\end{infobox}

## Sidebar Filters

The left sidebar contains all filtering controls. Filters are cumulative --- unchecking items in any category restricts all analyses.

### Core Filters

- **Genotype**: Check/uncheck groups to include (e.g., WT, KO)
- **Identity**: Check/uncheck cell populations
- **Cell Cycle**: Check/uncheck phases (G0/G1, S, G2/M, M)

### Additional Metadata Filters

Any optional metadata columns (timepoint, condition, cell\_type) automatically appear as additional filter sections.

### Comparison Variable

The **Comparison Variable** dropdown selects which column is used as the primary grouping axis for all statistical tests. Default is `genotype`.

### Reference Level

The **Reference Level** dropdown selects the baseline group for statistical comparisons (e.g., WT as reference when comparing WT vs KO).

### Special Filters (Dynamic)

Two additional filter sections appear when activated:

- **Quadrant Gate Population**: After running quadrant gating, filter all analyses to specific gated populations
- **Cluster Identity**: After naming clusters, filter by cluster identity

### Applying Filters

After adjusting any checkboxes, click **Apply Filters**. The filtered cell count updates in the summary bar. All tabs then analyze only the filtered subset.

\begin{tipbox}[Tip: Filter Strategy]
Start with all data to get an overview, then progressively filter to focus on specific populations, phases, or conditions. For example: first view all cells, then filter to G0/G1 only to see quiescent-state histone PTM differences.
\end{tipbox}

\newpage

# Exploration Tabs

## Overview

The Overview tab provides a bird's-eye view of your dataset after filtering:

- **Cell counts by genotype**: Total cells per comparison group
- **Cells by identity**: Distribution across populations
- **Cells by replicate**: Replicate balance check
- **Cell cycle distribution**: Phase proportions per genotype
- **Marker summaries**: Box-and-whisker plots for each H3-PTM and phenotypic marker

This is the first place to look for data quality issues --- unbalanced replicates, unexpected cell cycle distributions, or outlier markers.

## Ridge Plots

Kernel density distributions for a selected marker, overlaid by group.

**Controls:**

- **Marker**: Select which H3-PTM or phenotypic marker to plot
- **Group by**: Primary axis (genotype, identity, cell\_cycle, or any metadata column)
- **Color by**: Color the densities by a second variable

Ridge plots are ideal for comparing distribution shapes --- bimodality, shifts, or spread differences that summary statistics miss.

## Violin Plots

Grouped violin plots with embedded box plots, median lines, and optional inline statistics.

**Controls:**

- **Marker**: Select marker
- **Group by**: Primary grouping variable

When LMM results are available (from the Statistics tab), violin plots display p-values and Cohen's d effect sizes directly on the plot.

## Heatmap

Clustered mean-expression heatmap across all H3-PTMs.

**Controls:**

- **Group by**: Choose how rows are organized

The heatmap shows all markers simultaneously, making it easy to spot coordinate changes in the epigenetic landscape across conditions.

\newpage

# Statistical Analysis

## Linear Mixed Models (LMM)

The primary statistical method in EpiFlow D3. Accessible from the **Statistics** tab.

### Model

For each marker, EpiFlow fits:

$$\text{value} \sim \text{group} + (1|\text{replicate})$$

This accounts for the nested structure of cells within biological replicates, avoiding pseudoreplication. P-values use Satterthwaite degrees of freedom and are corrected using Benjamini-Hochberg FDR.

### Running LMM

1. Go to the **Statistics** tab
2. Select a single marker for detailed results, or click **Run All Markers** for a comprehensive analysis
3. Optionally set **Stratify by** to run separate models within subgroups (e.g., per cell cycle phase)

### Interpreting Results

- **Estimate**: Difference in means between groups
- **Std. Error**: Precision of the estimate
- **p-value**: Significance after FDR correction
- **Cohen's d**: Effect size magnitude

\begin{warningbox}[Pseudoreplication]
Never use a standard t-test on single-cell data --- cells from the same replicate are not independent observations. EpiFlow's LMM accounts for this by modeling replicate as a random effect. This is the statistically correct approach for nested biological data.
\end{warningbox}

## Forest Plot

Visualizes effect sizes with 95\% confidence intervals across all markers simultaneously. Generated from the **Run All Markers** results. Markers are sorted by effect size, making it easy to identify the strongest responders.

**Stratification**: Use the stratify dropdown to generate separate forest plots per cell cycle phase, identity, or other variable.

## Volcano Plot

Log$_2$ fold-change vs. $-$log$_{10}$ adjusted p-value. Markers in the upper corners are both statistically significant and biologically meaningful. Threshold lines indicate significance and fold-change cutoffs.

## Cell Cycle Analysis

The **Cell Cycle** tab provides phase-specific marker comparisons:

- **Phase distribution**: Stacked bar chart and summary heatmap of cell cycle proportions per genotype
- **Per-phase markers**: Select a phase to see marker expression within that phase only
- **Phase-resolved heatmap**: H3-PTM expression across all phases and genotypes

\newpage

# Advanced Analysis

## Correlation Analysis

Marker-marker Pearson correlation matrices, computed per group.

- **Full matrix**: Correlation heatmap with hierarchical clustering
- **Differential correlation**: Tests whether correlations differ between groups --- identifies rewired epigenetic relationships

\begin{tipbox}[When to Use Differential Correlation]
Correlation rewiring is often more informative than mean differences. Two groups may have similar mean H3K27ac but completely different H3K27ac--H3K4me1 correlations, indicating altered co-regulation.
\end{tipbox}

## Positivity Analysis

Gaussian Mixture Model (GMM) thresholding determines positive/negative status for each marker per cell. Fraction-positive values are computed per replicate, and group comparisons use replicate-level t-tests.

## Quadrant Gating

Biaxial gating on two selected markers with customizable thresholds and population labels.

1. Select X and Y markers (H3-PTMs or phenotypic)
2. Adjust threshold crosshairs
3. Label each quadrant (e.g., H3K27ac+ H3K4me1+)
4. Click **Apply as Metacolumn** to use populations as a filter/grouping variable across all tabs

## PCA

Principal Component Analysis on per-cell marker profiles.

- **2D PCA**: Interactive scatter plot colored by genotype, identity, or cell cycle
- **3D PCA**: Rotatable 3D scatter (Three.js)
- **Loadings**: Which markers drive each principal component

## UMAP

Nonlinear dimensionality reduction with adjustable parameters:

- **n\_neighbors**: Local neighborhood size (default 15)
- **min\_dist**: Minimum distance between points (default 0.1)
- **metric**: Distance metric (euclidean, cosine, manhattan)

## Clustering

Unsupervised cell grouping with multiple algorithms:

| Method | Description |
|--------|-------------|
| **K-means** | Fast, assumes spherical clusters |
| **Hierarchical** | Dendrogram-based (Ward, complete, average linkage) |
| **Louvain** | Graph-based community detection |
| **Leiden** | Improved graph-based (recommended for large datasets) |

### Workflow

1. Choose method and number of clusters (or use the **Elbow Plot** to determine optimal k)
2. Run clustering
3. View UMAP-embedded cluster scatter plot and cross-tabulation
4. **Rename clusters**: Enter biologically meaningful names (e.g., "Stem Cells", "Progenitors")
5. **Apply names**: Cluster identities become available as a grouping/stratification variable across all tabs and appear as a sidebar filter

\begin{infobox}[Cluster Identities Propagate]
After applying cluster names, a new \textbf{Cluster Identity} option appears in all group-by, color-by, stratify-by, and comparison variable dropdowns across every tab. You can generate ridge plots grouped by cluster identity, run LMMs stratified by cluster, or filter the entire analysis to specific clusters.
\end{infobox}

\newpage

# Machine Learning

## Random Forest

Classification with train/test split, feature importance ranking, ROC/AUC, and confusion matrices.

1. Go to the **ML** tab
2. Select features (H3-PTMs and/or phenotypic markers)
3. Click **Run Random Forest**
4. Review: accuracy, OOB error, feature importance bar chart, ROC curve, confusion matrix

\begin{infobox}[Automatic Subsampling]
For datasets larger than 50,000 cells, EpiFlow automatically subsamples before fitting the model. This prevents memory issues on the server while preserving statistical power --- 50K cells is more than sufficient for robust classification.
\end{infobox}

## Gradient Boosted Models (GBM)

XGBoost-based classification with the same interface as Random Forest. GBM often achieves higher accuracy on complex epigenetic signatures. Also automatically subsampled at 50K cells.

## Epigenetic Signatures

Group-specific H3-PTM signature extraction with z-score heatmaps. Identifies the marker profile that defines each group --- useful for biomarker discovery and diagnostic assessment.

## Diagnostic Assessment

Comprehensive multivariate profiling:

- **MANOVA**: Tests whether groups differ across all markers simultaneously
- **LDA**: Linear Discriminant Analysis projection
- **LMM consistency matrix**: Cross-marker significance patterns
- **Stratified signatures**: Signatures within cell cycle phases or other strata
- **K-means validation**: Whether unsupervised clustering recovers known groups

\newpage

# Exporting Results

## Figure Export

Every chart in EpiFlow D3 has export buttons in the top-right corner:

- **SVG**: Vector format for publication (scalable, editable in Illustrator/Inkscape)
- **PNG (2x)**: High-resolution raster at 2x device pixel ratio

## Figure Composer

Assemble multi-panel figures directly in EpiFlow:

1. Generate individual plots across tabs
2. Open the **Figure Composer**
3. Select grid layout (1x1 through 3x3)
4. Drag plots into panels
5. Panel labels (A, B, C...) are added automatically
6. Export as a single SVG or PNG

## CSV Tables

Every statistical result has a **Download CSV** button:

- LMM results (estimates, p-values, effect sizes)
- Positivity fractions
- Gating assignments
- Correlation matrices
- ML feature importances
- Diagnostic assessments

## HTML Report

Click **Generate Report** to create a comprehensive HTML report containing all active visualizations and statistics from the current session.

## Session Save/Load

- **Save Session**: Downloads a JSON file capturing all current settings, filters, and analysis state
- **Load Session**: Restores a previous session for reproducibility

\begin{tipbox}[Reproducibility]
Always save your session after completing an analysis. The JSON file records every filter, parameter, and threshold --- anyone can reload it to reproduce your exact analysis.
\end{tipbox}

\newpage

# Tips and Best Practices

## Analysis Workflow Recommendations

1. **Start with Overview**: Check data quality, replicate balance, and cell cycle distributions
2. **Filter progressively**: Begin with all cells, then narrow by phase or population
3. **Run All Markers LMM first**: This gives you the statistical foundation for interpreting all other plots
4. **Use stratification**: Run LMMs stratified by cell cycle to find phase-specific effects
5. **Check correlations**: Differential correlation often reveals biology that mean comparisons miss
6. **Validate with ML**: If LMMs show significant differences, RF/GBM confirms how separable the groups are
7. **Name your clusters**: Always assign biological identities to clusters before exporting

## Statistical Considerations

- **Replicate requirement**: LMMs need $\geq$ 2 replicates per group. With only 1 replicate, the random effect cannot be estimated.
- **Multiple testing**: All p-values are FDR-corrected across markers. Report adjusted p-values.
- **Effect sizes**: Cohen's d $<$ 0.2 is small, 0.2--0.5 medium, $>$ 0.8 large. Always report effect sizes alongside p-values.
- **Subsampling**: ML models subsample to 50K cells. This does not affect LMMs, correlation, or visualization --- only RF, GBM, and diagnostic assessment.

## Performance Tips

- **Large datasets** (100K+ cells): Plots may take a few seconds. UMAP and clustering are the slowest operations.
- **Browser**: Chrome or Firefox recommended. Safari works but may be slower for 3D plots.
- **Session timeout**: The server keeps your data in memory. If you leave the page for an extended time, you may need to re-upload.

\newpage

# License

EpiFlow D3 is open-source software licensed under the **GNU Affero General Public License v3.0** (AGPL-3.0). You are free to use, modify, and redistribute this software under the terms of the AGPL-3.0. Any modified versions deployed as a web service must also be released under AGPL-3.0 with full source code.

All copies, substantial portions, and derivative works must retain the original copyright notice and the following attribution:

\begin{quote}
EpiFlow D3 was originally developed by Dr.\ Maria A.\ Serrano at the Serrano Lab, Center for Regenerative Medicine (CReM), Boston University.
\end{quote}

Source code: \textcolor{oiblue}{https://github.com/SerranoLab/epiflow-d3}

\newpage

# Citation

If you use EpiFlow D3 in your research, please cite:

\begin{quote}
EpiFlow D3: A spectral flow cytometry analysis platform for multiparametric histone H3 post-translational modification profiling. Serrano Lab, Center for Regenerative Medicine (CReM), Boston University. https://epiflow.serranolab.org
\end{quote}

## Methods Paragraph

\begin{quote}
Spectral flow cytometry data were analyzed using EpiFlow D3 v1.0 (Serrano Lab, Center for Regenerative Medicine, Boston University; https://epiflow.serranolab.org). Multiparametric histone H3 post-translational modification (PTM) profiles were measured per cell. Statistical comparisons between groups were performed using linear mixed models (LMM; value $\sim$ group + (1|replicate)) to account for cell-level nesting within biological replicates. P-values were corrected using the Benjamini-Hochberg procedure. Effect sizes are reported as Cohen's d. Marker positivity was determined via Gaussian Mixture Model (GMM) thresholding with replicate-level fraction-positive t-tests for inference.
\end{quote}

## Contact

**Maria Serrano, PhD**\
Serrano Laboratory\
Center for Regenerative Medicine (CReM)\
Boston University School of Medicine\
Email: maserr@bu.edu\
Web: https://serranolab.github.io/online/

\vfill

\begin{center}
\textcolor{mediumgray}{\footnotesize Document Version 1.0 | EpiFlow D3 v1.0 | Last Updated 2025}
\end{center}
